questions.question: In Spring Boot 2.5+, when does a class with a single constructor NOT need @Autowired?
options: Only when the constructor has exactly one parameter
Always — Spring automatically injects through the single constructor without @Autowired
Only when the class is annotated with @Service (not @Component)
Only when all constructor parameters are interfaces

questions.question: What extra behavior does @Repository add compared to @Component?
options: It automatically generates CRUD methods without any code
It registers the class with the JPA entity manager
It translates platform-specific database exceptions into Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy
It creates a connection pool for the annotated class

questions.question: Spring finds two beans implementing the same PaymentGateway interface. You inject PaymentGateway without @Qualifier. What happens?
options: Spring randomly picks one of the two beans
Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException at startup
Spring injects both beans into a List<PaymentGateway>
Spring ignores both beans and injects null

questions.question: Why is programming to an interface (e.g., NotificationService) rather than a concrete class (EmailNotificationService) important for Spring DI?
options: Spring can only inject interface types, not class types
Interfaces compile faster than classes in Java
You can swap implementations (mock, email, SMS) without changing the class that uses them
Spring requires interfaces to generate proxy objects for every injection point

questions.question: What is the primary reason to prefer constructor injection over field injection with @Autowired?
options: Constructor injection runs faster at startup
Field injection requires more XML configuration
Constructor injection allows writing unit tests by calling new MyService(mock1, mock2) without a Spring context
Field injection is deprecated since Spring Boot 3.0
